message.content:
The bell above the door jingled, cutting through the symphony of ticking that filled the shop. It wasn’t a single sound, but a layered rhythm—hundreds of clocks, each with its own heartbeat, creating a chaotic yet harmonious hum.

Leo, a boy of about eight with knees stained from grass and pockets full of pebbles, stepped inside. He blinked, adjusting to the dim, amber light. Behind a counter cluttered with gears, springs, and magnifying glasses sat Mr. Abernathy. The old man looked as though he had been carved from the same oak as the grandfather clocks lining the walls. His fingers were stained with oil, and his eyes, magnified by thick spectacles, were focused on the inside of a pocket watch.

"Mr. Abernathy?" Leo asked, his voice barely rising above the ticking.

The clockmaker didn’t look up immediately. He used a pair of tweezers to place a gear no larger than a grain of rice into place. "Aye, young man. If you’re here to buy a watch, I’m afraid I only repair them. If you’re here to steal one, I’m afraid I have a very loud dog who sleeps under the counter."

Leo smiled, stepping closer. "I don’t want to buy anything. And I don’t want to steal. I just... I have a question."

Mr. Abernathy finally looked up, wiping his hands on a rag. "Questions are dangerous things. They have a way of winding up tight and snapping if you’re not careful. What is it?"

"Is time real?" Leo asked. "Or is it just something we made up so we don’t get lost?"

The old man chuckled, a dry, rattling sound. He picked up a brass pendulum from the counter and let it swing gently.
